title: Repetition and Loops in Generation
concept: A major failure mode: generative models get stuck in repetitive word loops ('the the the'), requiring penalties during decoding.

exercises.prompt: What is a common failure mode for generative AI models during text generation?

options.text: Getting stuck in repetitive loops of words

exercises.explain: When AI models generate text, they can get caught in infinite loops of repeating words like 'the the the'. We need a way to penalize repeats to keep the text natural.

cards.title: Stuck on Repeat!
cards.body: Have you ever heard someone repeat the same word over and over when they get nervous? AI models do the exact same thing! Without special rules, generative models easily get trapped in infinite word loops, saying things like 'the the the' forever. Let's find out why this happens and how we stop it.

cards.title: What is a Repetition Loop?
cards.body: A repetition loop happens when a generative AI model gets stuck in a feedback loop. It predicts a word, and because that word is now in its recent history, it becomes even more likely to predict it again. This leads to endless repetition like 'and then and then and then...'. To prevent this, we apply a 'repetition penalty' during decoding, which artificially lowers the score of words that have already been used.

cards.title: The Scratched Record
cards.body: Imagine a record player with a deep scratch. When the needle hits the scratch, it plays the same second of music over and over again: 'spin, spin, spin...' Generative AI does the same thing when it falls into a loop. To fix the record, you need a physical nudge—just like an AI model needs a 'repetition penalty' nudge to force it to move on to the next word.

cards.title: Loops in Action
cards.body:
Without a repetition penalty, a model generating a recipe might output:
'First, chop the onions, chop the onions, chop the onions...'

With a repetition penalty applied:
'First, chop the onions, heat the pan, and sauté them until golden.'

cards.title: Myth: Smart Models Don't Loop
cards.body: It is easy to assume that if an AI is smart, it won't repeat itself. However, even the most advanced LLMs naturally fall into repetitive loops because of how math and probability work during text generation. Repetition is a mathematical trap, not a sign of a 'dumb' model.

cards.title: With vs. Without Penalty
cards.body: See how applying a penalty changes the probability of words during generation, helping the AI escape repetitive traps.

left.text: The model sees 'the' in its output history, increases its probability, and outputs 'the the the...' forever.

right.text: The model sees 'the' in its history, applies a penalty to lower its score, and chooses a new word instead.

exercises.prompt: Why do generative models get stuck in repetitive word loops?

options.text: The model repeatedly predicts the same words based on its own recent output history.
The user keeps typing the same prompt over and over.
The model runs out of computer memory and has to restart the sentence.
The internet connection drops midway through generating text.

exercises.explain: When a word is generated, its presence in the text history makes the AI mathematically favor it again. This creates a loop where the same words keep winning the prediction race.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Without penalties, generative models are highly prone to getting stuck repeating the same phrases.
exercises.explain: Repetition loops are a common failure mode in decoding, which is why repetition penalties are a standard setting in modern text generation.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to complete the sentence about preventing AI loops.
exercises.sentence: To prevent an AI from getting stuck in a loop, we apply a repetition [blank] during decoding.
exercises.explain: A repetition penalty reduces the chance of selecting words that have already been generated, steering the AI toward fresh vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs most likely shows an AI model stuck in a repetition loop?

options.text: 'The weather tomorrow will be sunny with a high of 75 degrees.'
'This movie was very very very very very very good.'
'I love cooking pasta, especially when it has fresh garlic.'

exercises.explain: AI text that repeats words like 'very very very' in a robotic, loop-like fashion is a classic sign of a generative model failing to use a repetition penalty.

exercises.prompt: You are building a chatbot and notice it keeps answering 'Yes, I agree, I agree, I agree...'. How does adding a repetition penalty solve this?

options.text: It deletes the repeated words after the generation is completely finished.
It lowers the score of already-used words so the AI is forced to pick new ones.
It speeds up the processing hardware to skip over repeated sentences.
It shuts down the model and restarts the prompt from scratch.

exercises.explain: A repetition penalty lowers the probability of words that have already been generated, which stops the AI from getting stuck in an infinite loop of repeating the same phrases.
